The Indian Ocean tsunami of December 2004 had 1 Introduction and scope far reaching consequences for agriculture in Aceh province, Indonesia, and particularly in Aceh Barat district, 150 km from the seaquake epicentre. In this study, the spatial distri- On 26 December 2004, a seaquake with magnitude of 9.3 on bution and temporal dynamics of soil and groundwater salin- the Richter scale close to the North Sumatran coast caused ity and their impact on tree crops were monitored in Aceh one of the most devastating tsunamis in recorded history. Barat from 2006 to 2008. On 48 sampling points along ten Worldwide, documented losses of human lives due to the transects, covering 40 km of coastline, soil and groundwa- disaster exceeded 250 000 and more than 1 million people ter salinity were measured and related to mortality and yield were displaced (Szczuciński et al., 2006). The province of depression of the locally most important tree crops. Given a Nanggroe Aceh Darussalam, and especially its district Aceh yearly rainfall of over 3000 mm, initial groundwater salin- Barat, only 150 km from the epicentre, was most severely ity declined rapidly from over 10 to less than 2 mS cm−1 affected by the consequences of the seismic shock and the within two years. On the other hand, seasonal dynamics of subsequent tsunami. Entire villages around the district capi- the groundwater table in combination with intrusion of saline tal Meulaboh were destroyed, and Shofiyati et al. (2005) re- water into the groundwater body led to recurring elevated ported that, out of 120 295 ha flooded land in Aceh province, salinity, sufficient to affect crops. Tree mortality and yield almost 19 000 ha were situated in the district of Aceh Barat. depression in the flooded area varied considerably between Analysis of satellite imagery showed that more than 17 % tree species. Damage to coconut (65 % trees damaged) was of over 200 000 ha agricultural land surveyed in Aceh and al- related to tsunami run-up height, while rubber (50 % trees most 25 % in Aceh Barat were affected by flooding due to the damaged) was mainly affected by groundwater salinity. These findings have implications on planting deep- Agriculture had been one of the most important sectors rooted tree crops as growth will be constrained by ongoing contributing to provincial employment and GDP before the oscillations of the groundwater table and salinity. tsunami (48 % and 32 %, respectively; CGI, 2005). The ma- jor crops grown by small-holders along the Acehnese west coast were paddy rice, vegetables, rubber, coconut, cocoa, fruit trees and, to a minor extent, oil palm (CGI, 2005). (left) Subsided land 200 m from the sea in Suak Nie, near Meulaboh, Aceh Barat, Indonesia, as consequence of the earthquake, and (right) tsunami mud deposits, 100 m from the seashore. crops, including natural vegetation such as Nypa fruticans, tsunami-related research was almost exclusively conducted contributed 15 to 45 % to the local pre-tsunami economy in on annual crops like paddy rice and vegetables (Agus et the four coastal subdistricts of Aceh Barat (Joshi et al., 2007). al., 2008). In contrast, trees are characterized by deep root- First projections after the disaster, usually based on remote ing systems, which make them more susceptible to soil sensing data and on electric conductivity (EC) measurements and groundwater salinity, even though many trees dispose in shallow wells for drinking water, predicted dismal per- of detoxification by mechanisms like leaf shedding. It was spectives for agriculture in the province. Mid- to long-term assumed that tree growth was equally influenced by both reduction in crop productivity was expected even in mod- groundwater and mud salinity (FAO, 2005; McLeod et al., erately affected districts (CGI, 2005), and large areas were 2010), whereas annuals were more affected by mud. Initially, deemed to be permanently lost for agricultural use (Verrelst farmers’ reports on tree mortality and crop failure seemed and Schaepman, 2007). Two main mechanisms were sup- to confirm these concerns. Although tree crop damage was posed to affect plants: (a) fast salinization caused by seawa- widely evident in the landscape, underlying causes were not ter inundation at the time of the tsunami
